label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking which of the options you cannot use to judge an Englishman's social class according to the passage. The passage says that most British people know their class by the way they speak, their clothes, their interests, or the type of food they eat. So these are the things that are indicators of social class. The question is about what you cannot judge by. The options are A) where he was born, B) the way he speaks, C) clothes, D) interests. The passage mentions that upper class people are not just rich but also come from a particular family, have the right friends, went to a certain school, and have the right accent. It also says that some poor people don't see themselves as working class because of their family background, education, etc. So family background is a factor, but where he was born? The passage doesn't mention birthplace as a factor. It talks about family, education, school, accent, etc., but not birthplace. So the answer should be A) where he was born, because the passage doesn't say that's a factor. The other options (B, C, D) are all mentioned in the passage as things that indicate social class. The
